The Cosmobiologist

That "Two Times e to the Two-i-pi" Thing from Project Hail Mary, Explained

A romp through number theory, geometry and trigonometry, the unit circle, Euler's formula, complex numbers, and more to find the cube roots of eight

Graham Lau
Feb 16, 2026
Comments

“What’s two plus two?” [...] “What’s the cube root of eight?” the computer asks. “Two times e to the two-i-pi.” “Incorrect. What’s the cube root of eight?” But I wasn’t incorrect.

The Astrobiology and Panzoic Book Club is reading Andy Weir’s phenomenal book, Project Hail Mary, this month.

It’s been so much fun reading this book together and getting even more hyped for the film.

So what the heck is this whole “two times e to the two-i-pi” thing and why is that also a correct answer?

“two times e to the two-i-pi”

The character’s answer is correct. Two times e to the two-i-pi, which can be written as 2e2πi, is a cube root of eight.

TL;DR:

  • Saying 2e2πi is actually the same thing as saying 2
  • By Euler’s Formula, e2πi = 1, so then 2e2πi = 2·1 = 2
  • You might know that a solution to x³ = 8 is that x=2, but there are actually three solutions when we consider complex numbers
  • 2e2πi is one of the three complex cubed roots of 8, with the other two being 2e2πi/3 and 2e4πi/3

Math time!

We’re going to start from first principles here and then work our way out. There’s a lot of math here, and it might get confusing.

Types of Numbers

There are many different types of numbers that we consider in mathematics and science.
